caption: One trip to Italy is never enough. @StanleyTucci returns for a second season of #TucciinItaly to explore 5 new regions, diving deeper into the food, people and traditions that define Italy. Don't miss the all-new season of #TucciInItaly, premiering May 11 at 9/8c on @natgeotv. Streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu. by @natgeo

This Earth Month, step into wonder with National Geographic as we celebrate everything that makes our planet so extraordinary 🌍Stream the National Geographic #EarthMonth collection all April long on @DisneyPlus. #StepIntoWonder by @natgeo

When was the last time you thanked a bee? Bees look out for all of us—they pollinate crops and are responsible for a third of the food we eat—so it’s our responsibility to look out for the bees in return. Happy Earth Day!Learn more about why we should bee grateful for bees by watching #SecretsOfTheBees, streaming now on @DisneyPlus and @hulu. by @natgeo

There’s wonder above, below, and all around us. The salt flats of Bolivia blur the line between Earth and the cosmos. 🌍💫 #StepIntoWonder #WelcomeToEarthSeries is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu. by @natgeo

Getting to see bees up close is always exciting—but for @drsammygrams, watching how broomstick bees protect their food for the next generation was truly unforgettable.#StepIntoWonder with National Geographic’s #EarthMonth collection throughout April and stream #SecretsOfTheBees on @DisneyPlus and @hulu. by @natgeo

Presented by National Geographic Society in partnership with Rolex.Antarctica might seem a frozen, lifeless place at first glance, but marine biologist and National Geographic Explorer Cristian Lagger is uncovering a very different story just offshore. On recent deep-sea expeditions in the Southern Ocean aboard the Schmidt Ocean Institute’s research vessel, Falkor (too), Lagger’s team has found thriving ecosystems deep below the surface—where icefish guard their eggs, octopuses drift through the currents, and colorful sponges and sea stars cover the seafloor.Rolex and the National Geographic Society are partnering on science-based expeditions to explore, study, and document change in the planet’s unique regions. Discover more about Cristian Lagger’s work at the link in bio. #PerpetualPlanetPhotograph by Cristian Lagger (@laggercristian) by @natgeo

This Earth Month, Maejor partnered with National Geographic for his new album Earth Moods: Frequencies (Vol. 1). With a track designed for restoration and clarity, take in the beauty of the ocean and its residents.Listen to @maejor's album wherever you stream music now and watch our #EarthMoods livestream throughout Earth Month on Nat Geo's YouTube channel at the link in bio. by @natgeo

Photographer @emmanuel.tardy photographed this brown-throated sloth after it caused a traffic jam while crossing a busy road in El Tanque, Costa Rica, where development is fragmenting habitats. “The moment is etched in my memory,” he says, “watching this sloth moving slowly across land that was no longer truly its own.” by @natgeo

Scholars have long known that William Shakespeare bought a home in London’s Blackfriars precinct in 1613, three years before his death. Now we finally know exactly where it was. The discovery of a 1668 floorplan of Blackfriars residences in the London Archives also helped shed light on Shakespeare's final years and revealed new insights into the drama over who would inherit the property.Learn more about Shakespeare's home at the link in bio. Photograph by Archivio GBB/Contrasto/Redux by @natgeo
Trapped in a flooded cave, and forced to endure extreme cold as they wait for a rescue, the clock is ticking for these adventurers.#ExtremeRescues is now streaming on @hulu. by @natgeo
